title: 03_filter_ny_adults.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Filter Two Conditions at Once

The campaign team needs customers who are **both** over 30 **and** living in New York. You wrote what reads like plain English: keep the rows where `age > 30 and city == "NY"`.

pandas refuses with a `ValueError` about the "truth value of a Series" being ambiguous. The English word `and` isn't what pandas wants for combining column conditions. Swap it for the right operator (and mind the parentheses) so the two filters apply together.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd

customers = pd.read_csv("customers.csv")

# Customers over 30 who live in New York
ny_adults = customers[customers.age > 30 and customers.city == "NY"]
print(ny_adults[["name", "age", "city"]])
print("count:", len(ny_adults))
traceback:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 6
      5 # Customers over 30 who live in New York
----> 6 ny_adults = customers[customers.age > 30 and customers.city == "NY"]

ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous. Use a.empty, a.bool(), a.item(), a.any() or a.all().
correctOutput:
name  age city
1   Marcus   41   NY
4    Priya   37   NY
6     Dana   52   NY
count: 3

options.label: Combine the masks with & and wrap each condition in parentheses
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

customers = pd.read_csv("customers.csv")

# Customers over 30 who live in New York
ny_adults = customers[(customers.age > 30) & (customers.city == "NY")]
print(ny_adults[["name", "age", "city"]])
print("count:", len(ny_adults))
options.resultLog:
name  age city
1   Marcus   41   NY
4    Priya   37   NY
6     Dana   52   NY
count: 3
options.rationale: Each comparison produces a whole column of True/False values (a boolean Series). Python's `and` only works on a single True/False, so it can't combine two columns and raises the ambiguity error. pandas uses `&` for element-by-element AND. The parentheses are required because `&` binds tighter than `>` and `==`, so without them the comparisons would be grouped wrong.

options.label: Just swap "and" for "&" and leave the rest alone
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

customers = pd.read_csv("customers.csv")

# Customers over 30 who live in New York
ny_adults = customers[customers.age > 30 & customers.city == "NY"]
print(ny_adults[["name", "age", "city"]])
print("count:", len(ny_adults))
options.resultLog:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 6
----> 6 ny_adults = customers[customers.age > 30 & customers.city == "NY"]

TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for &: 'int' and 'str'
options.rationale: `&` is the right operator but it has *higher* precedence than `>` and `==`, so Python reads this as `customers.age > (30 & customers.city) == "NY"`. It tries to AND the number 30 with the text city column and blows up. You must parenthesize each condition: `(customers.age > 30) & (customers.city == "NY")`.

options.label: Filter by age first, then filter that result by city
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd

customers = pd.read_csv("customers.csv")

# Customers over 30 who live in New York
adults = customers[customers.age > 30]
ny_adults = adults[customers.city == "NY"]
print(ny_adults[["name", "age", "city"]])
print("count:", len(ny_adults))
options.resultLog:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexingError                             Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 7
----> 7 ny_adults = adults[customers.city == "NY"]

pandas.errors.IndexingError: Unalignable boolean Series provided as indexer (index of the boolean Series and of the indexed object do not match).
options.rationale: The second mask `customers.city == "NY"` is built from the *full* table, so it has one True/False per original row. But you apply it to `adults`, which has fewer rows and a different index, so pandas can't line them up. Two-step filtering works only if both masks come from the same frame — cleaner to combine them with `&` in one step.

hints: Each comparison like `customers.age > 30` is not a single yes/no — it's a whole column of yes/no values. Python's `and` only knows how to combine one yes/no with another, which is why it complains.
pandas uses `&` for "and" across columns and `|` for "or". Because those symbols grab their neighbors tightly, put parentheses around each condition: `(... ) & ( ...)`.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
A comparison on a column, like `customers.age > 30`, returns a **boolean Series**: one True/False for every row. Python's keyword `and` is defined only for a single True/False value, so when it meets a whole column it cannot decide and raises `ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous`.

### Why the fix is correct
pandas overloads the bitwise operators for element-wise logic: `&` means AND, `|` means OR, applied row by row. So `(customers.age > 30) & (customers.city == "NY")` produces a single combined mask that is True only where both conditions hold. The parentheses are not optional: `&` has higher precedence than `>` and `==`, so without them Python would try to evaluate `30 & customers.city` first and fail. One combined mask, built from one frame, also avoids index-alignment problems that creep in when you filter in separate steps.
recruiterReview: Solid — you recognized that a column comparison is a vector of booleans, not a single condition, which is the whole heart of filtering in pandas. Using `&` with parentheses is the idiom every analyst leans on daily. Forward tip: when a filter expression grows past two or three conditions, `df.query("age > 30 and city == 'NY'")` lets you write it in readable English and sidesteps the precedence traps entirely. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Filtering Rows on More Than One Condition

Selecting rows that match several conditions is bread-and-butter analysis — and the operators surprise nearly every beginner once.

### The idea of a boolean mask
In pandas you don't loop over rows. Instead you write a condition on a whole column, e.g. `df.age > 30`. That returns a **boolean Series**: a True/False value for each row, like `[False, True, False, ...]`. Putting that mask in brackets, `df[mask]`, keeps the rows where it is True. This is called **boolean filtering**.

### Why `and` fails
Python's `and`/`or` keywords expect a single True or False. A boolean Series has *many* values, so Python can't reduce it to one answer and raises `The truth value of a Series is ambiguous`. pandas instead uses the symbols `&` (and), `|` (or), and `~` (not), which combine masks **element by element**.

### Mind the parentheses
`&` and `|` bind more tightly than comparison operators, so you must wrap each condition:
```python
df[(df.age > 30) & (df.city == "NY")]   # correct
df[df.age > 30 & df.city == "NY"]       # WRONG: evaluates 30 & df.city first
```

### A tiny worked example
```python
df = pd.DataFrame({"age": [25, 41, 37], "city": ["NY", "NY", "LA"]})
df[(df.age > 30) & (df.city == "NY")]
#    age city
# 1   41   NY
```
Only the 41-year-old in NY satisfies both conditions.

### How to spot it
The error message `truth value of a Series is ambiguous` almost always means you wrote `and`/`or` where you needed `&`/`|`. A `TypeError` about `&` between an int and a str usually means you forgot the parentheses.

### Common beginner mistakes
- Writing `and`/`or` instead of `&`/`|`.
- Using `&`/`|` but dropping the parentheses around each condition.
- Filtering in two steps with masks built from different frames, so the indexes don't align.
- Forgetting `~mask` is how you negate ("not").

You'll hit this bug the first time you ask for rows matching two conditions at once.
